methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series A → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.0x) to $90M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Climate & Energy (found 0 same-stage, 0 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 9 same-sector peers exist in Climate & Energy, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Climate & Bio-Manufacturing

data.narrative:
Exowatt is a Series A–stage climate and energy company founded by Hannan Happi and Jack Abraham that has raised $90M to date. The broader Climate & Energy sector comprises one public comparable (LNZA) and eight private peers, including Helion, Redwood Materials, QuantumScape, KoBold Metals, and Electric Hydrogen. However, a narrow peer-median approach proved inapplicable due to insufficient same-stage or adjacent-stage comparables within the sector meeting minimum sample thresholds (zero datapoints at matching criteria).

The analysis applied a stage-progression methodology, using the $90M total raised as a valuation proxy and applying a 2.0x step-up multiplier typical of Series A to subsequent-stage transitions. This approach yielded an implied valuation of $180M. No other triangulation methods produced sufficient data to cross-validate this estimate; peer-median analysis was rejected because the nine identified Climate & Energy peers included no tight clustering at Exowatt's stage or tier.

The quantitative support for a valuation range of $153M (low) to $207M (high), with a median estimate of $180M, rests on a single method. Key uncertainties include the representativeness of the 2.0x multiplier for Exowatt's specific risk profile and capital efficiency, and the absence of direct peer benchmarking. Investors should supplement this analysis with operator diligence on unit economics, technology differentiation, and comparable exit multiples in energy infrastructure and materials science.
